petitioner_background: The petitioner is a gender, diversity, and inclusion specialist seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in business under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). She earned a master's degree in anthropology (with minors in sociology and gender studies) from a Berlin institution in 2004, and a master's degree in international affairs in 2005. She has approximately 17 years of work experience with several United Nations agencies as a liaison officer, consultant, program manager, and partnerships manager, and at the time of filing was employed as a digital partnerships manager in G-4 nonimmigrant status. She intends to continue working in the field of gender, inclusion, and diversity in the private sector, specifically the media and entertainment industry.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed eligibility based on her asserted review of advertising campaign submissions at the Ad Venture Student Competition 2018-2019, sponsored by the European Institute for Commercial Communications Education (EDCOM), which required competing teams to provide advertising campaigns raising awareness of gender-stereotyped marketing aimed at children. She submitted: (1) a March 22, 2019 email from E-H-, an education and training assistant with the European Association of Communications Agencies (EACA), providing her individual login details to the Ad Venture judging platform, with an attached jury information pack indicating jurors would receive 7-9 campaigns to judge between March 22 and April 8, 2019, scoring each submission out of 10 points across three criteria (research; strategy; and creativity and media); (2) an April 4, 2019 email from E-L- checking on her judging process; (3) an April 5, 2019 email from the petitioner to E-L- stating 'I will make sure to complete the judging in the next couple of days'; and (4) emails from E-L- dated April 26, 2019 and May 22, 2019 thanking 'Ad Venture Jurors' for their participation and announcing finalists and winner.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew the Director's favorable finding. To meet this criterion, a petitioner must show not only that she was invited to judge but that she actually participated in judging the work of others in the same or allied field. The emails submitted only demonstrated a reminder to conduct judging and the petitioner's statement of intention to complete the task, but did not demonstrate that she actually completed judging advertising campaigns. The petitioner presented no supporting evidence establishing she actually performed the advertising campaign judging, and did not submit corroborating documentation showing how many or which advertising campaigns she reviewed in the first round.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner emphasized having contributed to and co-authored major professional publications including annual reports published in 2021. She submitted support letters from coworkers describing her contributions during her tenure as 'Gender Socialization Programme Manager' and 'Partnerships Manager' with a U.N. Gender Section in New York between 2017 and 2021. A letter from L-R- (Associate Director, Gender Equality) described her work researching and reporting how the COVID-19 pandemic impacted women and girls in three 2021 publications, and her development of corporate partnerships (one on self-esteem and body confidence for girls, another on STEM and digital literacy skills for girls). A letter from S-J- (Senior Advisor, Gender Equality) cited her contributions to additional 2021 publications including ones on advancing positive gender norms and socialization, responsive parenting, promoting diversity and inclusion, and a technical note on gender in advertising. She also submitted her claimed publications and a Google Scholar citation report for her 2021 publication 'Family-Friendly Policies for Workers in the Informal Economy' showing it was published on Papers.ssrn.com and Europepmc.org, plus several examples of articles citing that publication (including self-citations in other U.N. publications).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters described her experience and valuable contributions but did not describe a specific original contribution that impacted the broader field of gender, diversity, and inclusion, provoked widespread commentary, or influenced subsequent work. The letters from L-R- and S-J- did not elaborate on how her research contributions and reporting were of major significance in the overall field beyond the U.N. agencies that utilized her services. S-J-'s broad claims that her work was 'far-reaching and significant' did not provide specific information explaining how her contributions impacted the greater field. L-R- speculated on potential future influence (e.g., STEM partnership increasing representation in future careers), which was prospective rather than demonstrating actual major significance. On citations, the Google Scholar report showed the publication garnered zero citations, and the other five claimed publications were not listed. Citation examples submitted showed her work was used as background information rather than singled out as particularly important, and included self-citations. The AAO cited Kazarian v. USCIS (9th Cir. 2009) that publications and presentations are insufficient absent evidence of major significance, and noted USCIS need not accept primarily conclusory statements per 1756, Inc. v. U.S. Att'y Gen.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence that the petitioner performed in a leading or critical role for an organization with a distinguished reputation, including her roles with U.N. agencies.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that the petitioner performed in a leading or critical role for an organization with a distinguished reputation and satisfied this criterion under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii). This was the only criterion the AAO found the petitioner met on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner did not initially claim this criterion. After the Director's second RFE requested documentation of annual earnings and comparative wage evidence, she submitted evidence showing her salary in her current role as 'Digital Partnerships Manager' (also described by her employer as 'Partnerships & Fundraising Specialist'). For comparison, she offered wage data relating to salaries of gender, diversity, and inclusion consultants, specialists, and managers.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO withdrew the Director's favorable finding. The petitioner provided wage data for occupations that were not the same as her actual positions (digital partnerships manager / partnerships and fundraising specialist). Comparing her salary to gender, diversity, and inclusion consultants/specialists/managers did not demonstrate she commands a high salary 'in relation to others in the field' of her actual role. The AAO cited Matter of Price (comparing professional golfer to other PGA Tour golfers), Skokos v. U.S. Dept. of Homeland Sec. (salary for lesser duties not a valid comparison), Grimson v. INS (NHL enforcer versus other NHL enforcers), and Muni v. INS (NHL defenseman versus other NHL defensemen) to emphasize the need for field-specific comparisons.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner satisfied only one criterion (leading or critical role) rather than the required three under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The AAO withdrew the Director's favorable determinations on judging and high salary, and upheld the denial on original contributions. Although the petitioner claimed eligibility for an additional criterion on appeal relating to scholarly articles under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi), the AAO reserved that issue as unnecessary to the outcome. The AAO did not reach a formal final merits determination under Kazarian but noted that reviewing the record in the aggregate, it did not support a finding that the petitioner established the required acclaim and recognition, as the significance of her work was not indicative of sustained national or international acclaim or consistent with a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' as contemplated by Congress.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
1756, Inc. v. The U.S. Att'y Gen., 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.C. Dist. 1990)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
Skokos v. U.S. Dept. of Homeland Sec., 420 F. App'x 712 (9th Cir. 2011)
Grimson v. INS, 934 F. Supp. 965 (N.D. Ill. 1996)
Muni v. INS, 891 F. Supp. 440 (N.D. Ill. 1995)
